questions.question: Where should you typically create and add interactors for a CS106A GraphicsProgram?
options: A) init()
B) run()
C) main()
D) paintComponent()
questions.explanation: init() is for building the UI and registering listeners so controls are ready when run() starts.

questions.question: Which method is called when a JButton is clicked?
options: A) mouseClicked(MouseEvent e)
B) actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)
C) buttonClicked(ActionEvent e)
D) onClick()
questions.explanation: Buttons fire ActionEvents, which are handled by actionPerformed.

questions.question: To have a JTextField trigger an event when pressing Enter, you must:
options: A) Do nothing; it always fires automatically
B) Call addActionListeners() with no further setup
C) Register a listener on the field with textField.addActionListener(this)
D) Put the field in the CENTER region
questions.explanation: Text fields require an explicit ActionListener registration on the field (or equivalent).

questions.question: Which region creates a horizontal control strip at the bottom of the window?
options: A) NORTH
B) SOUTH
C) EAST
D) WEST
questions.explanation: SOUTH adds components in a strip across the bottom.

questions.question: What does e.getActionCommand() typically return for a JButton if not explicitly set?
options: A) The memory address of the button
B) The text label of the button
C) The region where the button was added
D) The class name of the button
questions.explanation: By default, the action command is the button’s label unless changed via setActionCommand.

questions.question: You need mutually exclusive color choices (Red/Blue/Green). Which setup is correct?
options: A) Three JCheckBoxes with no group
B) Three JRadioButtons with a ButtonGroup
C) One JComboBox and one JCheckBox
D) One JTextField listing colors
questions.explanation: Radio buttons become mutually exclusive when placed in a ButtonGroup.

questions.question: Which statement about event-driven programming is TRUE?
options: A) You must call actionPerformed manually when a user clicks
B) The framework calls your listener methods when events occur
C) Events can only come from the mouse, not from interactors
D) Listeners are optional; interactors update themselves
questions.explanation: Listeners are callbacks invoked by the framework upon user actions.

questions.question: Given: JButton add = new JButton("Add"); add(add, SOUTH); addActionListeners(); In actionPerformed, which is NOT a good way to identify this button?
options: A) if (e.getActionCommand().equals("Add"))
B) if (e.getSource() == add)
C) switch (e.getActionCommand()) { case "Add": ... }
D) if (e.getActionCommand() == "Add")
questions.explanation: Use .equals() to compare strings in Java; == compares references.

questions.question: What is the main reason to place long-running loops and animations in run() rather than init()?
options: A) init() cannot access interactors
B) init() is called repeatedly, run() only once
C) The window displays after init(), so run() won’t block showing of the GUI
D) init() cannot create components
questions.explanation: The GUI becomes visible after init(); running long tasks in init() can delay UI readiness.

questions.question: Which statement best describes how to place a label in the control strip and a shape on the canvas?
options: A) add(new JLabel("Hi"), CENTER); add(new GRect(10,10), SOUTH);
B) add(new JLabel("Hi"), SOUTH); add(new GRect(10,10));
C) add(new GRect(10,10), SOUTH); add(new JLabel("Hi"));
D) add(new JLabel("Hi")); add(new GRect(10,10), NORTH);
questions.explanation: Swing components go to regions (e.g., SOUTH). Graphics objects are added to the canvas with add(GObject).
